So sort of to beat up on this for one more minute or two, this is a very good example. So this is like your weekly facts, right, that goes to your coaching members. So what it says is it's finally here, new addition to the traffic guys members website, the market dominator, number one, the new members step by step guide page, don't get lost with all the tools. Okay, so that's what we got. Now you tell me what you said you wanted to accomplish. You wanted them to know, it was more of an announcement thing than anything. You wanted them to know it was available and that we had done a bunch of work on their behalf to develop these tools to help them get more traffic. Okay, so you wanted them to know that some WISBANG new tool was available that they didn't have before. And I asked the question, how do you want them to feel when they read this? What do you want? They're like visceral, right? Well, I want them to know we killed ourselves for them. You know, that we really worked to put this together and bring this to them. So did this do those two things? It's finally here, new additions to the traffic guys members website, the market dominator, number one, the new members step by step guide page, don't get lost with all the tools. Well, it touches the first objective. It misses the second objective altogether, doesn't it? Then get there. It needs the after 412 months of arduous labor in the hot, sweaty, burning volcano, we have emerged having spent $612,000 of capital on your behalf with the new WISBANG do-hickey that does all these things, right? So in his defense, he wants everybody to know this is I picked his worst facts. But I mean, it demonstrates the point perfectly, right? So we're clear here on these two things that we want to have happen. But then we didn't construct it in a way that it makes those two things happen. Why? Because for the most part, we don't give thought to what do we want to have happen. We just sat down and wrote. Right. Well, you got a few more interested in deadlines, time pressure, and get it out, getting it to them. The other reason it happens is under pressure, a lot of people are delegating what they view the lower level, less important media to people who don't get it, right? But what could be more important than the facts is to go to the people that give you the most amount of money and whether or not they're going to be retained or not, right? So I mean, in a way, I'd rather delegate the sales letter to sell something new than I would delegate to communications that are keeping everybody delighted. And that's what I learned out of this whole thing is that I have to ask the question, what do I want their response to be to this communication? Yep, what do we want them to know? How do we want them to feel? I mean, we know we want this sentence to get them to read the next sentence and all that stuff. That's foundational and mechanical. But now this is layered on top of it. What do we want them to instantly know? If we want them to be curious, what do we want to be curious about? How do we want them to feel? This is a, this affects profiling and language. So it's just a little example for you to take with you. This is a good idea, not particularly well executed. It comes from my friend, the late Bill Brooks. And what they did as a template, it's not bad, which is why I'm giving it to you. They went through in a bunch of different categories. And of course, they're mostly B2B guys. So I gave you the accountant who's the principal in the firm and one other one. I don't know what the O. The non-entrepreneurial CEO. And so these are people that somebody might want to sell to. And then they built in advance a language document of what are these people want? What words do they respond to positively? What words do they respond to negatively? What words you want to use? What words you don't want to use and so forth? Now, I don't think they like, I don't think they got the implementation of this done very well. But conceptually, they got it right. And it's a pretty good template. So for everybody you sell to, you could build a comparable resource tool for yourself that then goes with the czarist and the other things on the shelf to be checked as you are writing. Yep. But now ask him to write to accountants who run small businesses, you're in real trouble, because he don't know that animal at all. There's people at that table who need to, you know, get up. OK, here's my current. And it changes almost year to year and has for a number of years. But here is my current newsletter checklist. How many pages are there? Four. And then there is a sample newsletter with every item matched to the checklist. So I have alpha-coded everything, A, B, C, D, E, A, A, B, B, C, C, E, E, all the way through for you, which, of course, I don't work that way. But so I've taken my checklist, alpha-coded it for you. I've then taken a sample newsletter and found most of the examples of each of the things on the checklist as they are done in the newsletter. That makes the newsletter kind of a mess, because there's places where they overlap and there's two of them. But you'll get it. And let me just quickly take you through the checklist. So on the first page, there is the What's in This Issue box. And so what I'm usually trying to do in that box is, number one, of course, get them to read the damn newsletter or go somewhere in the newsletter and start reading. I don't care where, really. But so create readership. If we're given anything away, either enclosed, attached, or by goose chase that they get to go get it, I'll note it in that box. And if we're promoting something, I'll usually note it in that box. The quote box probably ought to be replaced with something to tell you the truth. So sometimes it's used for theme quotes. And sometimes we don't put quotes in there at all and we promote something in there.
